grammarRules.summary: The foundation of English. Used for habits, routines, permanent facts, and general truths.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Think of Present Simple as a photograph of your life in general — not what's happening this exact second, but how things ARE in the big picture. You live somewhere. You work somewhere. You drink coffee every morning. These are your 'default settings'. The key question to ask yourself: 'Is this always true, or does it happen regularly?' If yes — Present Simple is your tense. It describes the world as it is, not as it's changing.
grammarRules.structure: I/You/We/They + base verb | He/She/It + verb+(s/es) | Subject + don't/doesn't + base verb | Do/Does + subject + base verb?

usages.title: Habits and regular actions
usages.explanation: Anything you do on a regular basis — daily, weekly, every Monday, never. The action repeats. Signal words are your best clue here: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never, every day/week/morning, on Mondays, twice a week, at the weekend. If you see these words, reach for Present Simple automatically.

examples.sentence: I wake up at 6:30 every morning without an alarm.
examples.translation: This is a daily habit — it happens every single day.

examples.sentence: She checks her emails before she even gets out of bed.
examples.translation: A routine action — she does this regularly, always in this order.

examples.sentence: They never eat breakfast together on weekdays.
examples.translation: Never = 0% of the time — a permanent negative habit.

examples.sentence: He goes to the gym three times a week.
examples.translation: A fixed schedule — three times a week, always.

usages.title: Permanent facts and scientific truths
usages.explanation: Things that are always true — laws of nature, scientific facts, definitions, geography. These will be true tomorrow, next year, and in a hundred years. Nobody says 'The Earth is currently orbiting the Sun' — it just always does it.

examples.sentence: The Earth orbits the Sun once every 365 days.
examples.translation: A scientific fact — always true, no exceptions.

examples.sentence: Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at sea level.
examples.translation: A permanent physical fact — not a habit, just the way the universe works.

examples.sentence: Mammals give birth to live young.
examples.translation: A biological definition — permanently true.

examples.sentence: The Nile flows north into the Mediterranean Sea.
examples.translation: A geographical fact — it has always flowed this way.

usages.title: Permanent situations (how life is in general)
usages.explanation: Long-term facts about someone's life — where they live, what they do for work, what they believe. Not temporary changes, not what's happening right now — just the current state of affairs. This is where students often mix it with Present Continuous. Ask yourself: 'Is this my permanent life situation, or is it just for now?' If it's your life in general → Present Simple.

examples.sentence: I live in Berlin. My whole family lives there.
examples.translation: This is my permanent home — not a temporary stay.

examples.sentence: She works as a senior architect at a design firm.
examples.translation: Her job — a permanent situation, not something she's doing just for this week.

examples.sentence: He speaks three languages: English, French, and Arabic.
examples.translation: A skill he permanently has — it doesn't switch on and off.

examples.sentence: My parents own a small restaurant near the beach.
examples.translation: Ownership — a permanent state.

usages.title: Timetables and official schedules
usages.explanation: For transport, cinema, classes, sports events — things with a fixed, official schedule that doesn't change. This is a special use: it refers to the FUTURE but uses Present Simple because the schedule is fixed and unchangeable.

examples.sentence: The train to Edinburgh leaves at 9:15 from platform 3.
examples.translation: Official timetable — this is how it is, always, every day.

examples.sentence: The museum opens at 10 am and closes at 6 pm on Sundays.
examples.translation: Fixed official hours — not a plan, but an established fact.

examples.sentence: The conference starts on Monday and finishes on Wednesday.
examples.translation: Official event schedule — it's fixed, like a fact.

sections.title: Affirmative — Adding -(s/es) for he/she/it
sections.content: I/You/We/They: base verb, no changes. He/She/It: add -s to most verbs (work→works, play→plays, eat→eats). Add -es to verbs ending in -o, -ch, -sh, -x, -s (go→goes, watch→watches, wash→washes, fix→fixes, miss→misses). Verbs ending in consonant+y: change y to i and add -es (study→studies, try→tries, carry→carries). Two important irregular forms: have→has, be→is.

examples.sentence: I play / She plays | I go / He goes | I study / She studies
examples.translation: Notice how the verb changes only for he/she/it.

examples.sentence: He has a dog. / She is a teacher.
examples.translation: Irregular: have→has, be→is (not 'haves' or 'bes'!)

examples.sentence: My brother watches football every Saturday and never misses a game.
examples.translation: watch→watches, miss→misses — verbs ending in -ch and -ss get -es

sections.title: Negative — don't and doesn't
sections.content: I/You/We/They + don't (do not) + base verb. He/She/It + doesn't (does not) + base verb. CRITICAL RULE: After doesn't, the main verb is ALWAYS the base form — no -s, no -es, no changes! This is the #1 mistake students make. The 'doesn't' already contains the 3rd person signal, so the main verb stays pure.

examples.sentence: I don't eat meat. / He doesn't eat meat.
examples.translation: Both correct. Notice: 'doesn't eat' NOT 'doesn't eats'.

examples.sentence: She doesn't like spicy food at all.
examples.translation: Correct. 'doesn't like' — the verb stays in base form.

examples.sentence: They don't work on weekends.
examples.translation: don't for they/we/you/I

examples.sentence: It doesn't rain much in summer here.
examples.translation: it → doesn't — not 'don't'

sections.title: Questions — Do and Does
sections.content: Do + I/You/We/They + base verb? / Does + He/She/It + base verb? In a question, Do/Does 'steals' the third-person responsibility. So the main verb is ALWAYS base form in questions too. Think of it this way: Does already says 'third person singular' — the main verb doesn't need to repeat that.

examples.sentence: Do you like jazz? / Does she like jazz?
examples.translation: Both correct. 'Does she like' — not 'Does she likes'.

examples.sentence: Where do you work? / Where does he work?
examples.translation: WH-question: question word + do/does + subject + base verb

examples.sentence: What time does the film start?
examples.translation: Asking about a schedule/timetable — correct use of does

examples.sentence: How often do they visit you?
examples.translation: How often = asking about frequency → Present Simple

comparisons.explanation: This is the most important comparison in beginner English. Present Simple = ALWAYS, IN GENERAL, PERMANENTLY. Present Continuous = RIGHT NOW, TEMPORARILY, AT THIS MOMENT. The test: ask yourself 'Is this my general life situation, or is it something happening/changing right now?' General life → Simple. Right now or temporary → Continuous. Some verbs (stative verbs: know, want, love, need, understand, believe, own, seem) almost NEVER appear in Continuous — they only go in Simple.

examples.sentence: I live in London. (Simple) — this is my permanent home
examples.translation: General fact about my life.

examples.sentence: I'm living in London for 3 months. (Continuous) — temporary, I'll move back soon
examples.translation: Temporary situation — makes all the difference.

examples.sentence: She works at Google. (Simple) — her permanent job
examples.translation: This is her career, her permanent situation.

examples.sentence: She's working from home this week. (Continuous) — only this week, not always
examples.translation: Temporary arrangement — back in the office next week.

commonMistakes: She go to school every day. ✗ → She goes to school every day. ✓ (he/she/it needs -s)
He doesn't likes coffee. ✗ → He doesn't like coffee. ✓ (after doesn't, always base verb)
Do she work here? ✗ → Does she work here? ✓ (he/she/it needs Does, not Do)
I am knowing the answer. ✗ → I know the answer. ✓ (know is a stative verb — never Continuous)
Are you understanding me? ✗ → Do you understand me? ✓ (understand is stative)

grammarRules.summary: Used for actions happening right now, temporary situations, changing situations, and fixed future arrangements.
grammarRules.coreConcept: If Present Simple is a photograph of your life in general, Present Continuous is a live video recording. Something is HAPPENING — it's in progress, it's not finished yet. The key feeling is TEMPORARINESS and INCOMPLETENESS. The action is unfolding as you speak, or it's a temporary phase that will eventually end. Ask yourself: 'Is this happening at this exact moment, or is it a temporary phase of my life?' If yes to either — Present Continuous is your answer.

usages.title: Actions happening right now, at this moment
usages.explanation: The action is literally in progress as you speak. You can almost point at it. Signal words: now, right now, at the moment, at this moment, currently, Look!, Listen! These words are your green light to use Present Continuous without hesitation.

examples.sentence: I can't talk right now — I'm driving.
examples.translation: The driving is happening this very second — literally in progress.

examples.sentence: Listen! Someone is playing the piano beautifully.
examples.translation: Listen! = the sound is happening right now as you speak.

examples.sentence: Look — it's snowing! The ground is turning white.
examples.translation: Look! = you can see it happening before your eyes.

examples.sentence: She's not available — she's meeting with a client.
examples.translation: Right now, at this moment, the meeting is in progress.

usages.title: Temporary situations (not permanent)
usages.explanation: A situation that exists for a limited period — this week, this semester, these days. It's not your permanent life, it's a phase. This is where students confuse Simple and Continuous most. The crucial distinction: Simple = always, Continuous = for now, but not forever. Signal words: this week, this month, these days, at the moment, temporarily, currently, for now.

examples.sentence: I'm staying at my sister's place this week — my flat is being renovated.
examples.translation: Temporary! I don't always live there — just this week.

examples.sentence: She's learning to drive these days. It's going well.
examples.translation: A temporary phase — she's in the process of learning, not yet finished.

examples.sentence: He's working on a huge project this month and barely sleeps.
examples.translation: This month only — a temporary intense period.

examples.sentence: I'm reading a fascinating book about human psychology.
examples.translation: I'm in the middle of it — started but not finished yet.

usages.title: Changing and developing situations
usages.explanation: When things are in the process of CHANGING. Not suddenly, not all at once — but gradually shifting. This use doesn't have a specific moment — it describes a trend or evolution happening over time.

examples.sentence: The climate is getting warmer every decade.
examples.translation: A gradual ongoing change — it's happening over years.

examples.sentence: More and more young people are moving to cities.
examples.translation: A developing trend — it's changing, not finished.

examples.sentence: Technology is changing the way we communicate.
examples.translation: An ongoing transformation — it's in progress across society.

examples.sentence: Your English is really improving! Keep it up.
examples.translation: A gradual positive change that's still in progress.

usages.title: Fixed future arrangements (personal plans)
usages.explanation: You've already made concrete arrangements for the future — booked tickets, confirmed with someone, scheduled it. This is different from intentions (going to) and predictions (will). You have PROOF it's happening: a ticket, a calendar entry, a confirmed appointment. If you cancelled, someone would be disappointed.

examples.sentence: I'm meeting Sarah for lunch tomorrow at 1pm — she's booked the restaurant.
examples.translation: It's confirmed, organised — almost like it's already happening.

examples.sentence: We're flying to Tokyo next Monday. I've already packed.
examples.translation: Tickets bought, bags ready — it's definitely happening.

examples.sentence: She's starting her new job on the 1st. Everyone knows about it.
examples.translation: Officially arranged — her employer is expecting her.

examples.sentence: What are you doing this evening? — I'm having dinner with my parents.
examples.translation: A confirmed arrangement — they're expecting you.

usages.title: Annoying repeated actions (with 'always')
usages.explanation: When someone does something irritating or surprising repeatedly. Using 'always' with Continuous adds an emotional layer — frustration, annoyance, or amazement. This is NOT the same as Present Simple + always, which is neutral. Continuous + always = there's feeling behind it.

examples.sentence: He's always losing his keys! It drives me crazy.
examples.translation: Irritating habit — the speaker is frustrated. (Simple: 'He always loses his keys' = neutral fact)

examples.sentence: You're always interrupting me when I'm trying to explain something!
examples.translation: Annoyed complaint — happens repeatedly and bothers the speaker.

examples.sentence: She's always bringing me little gifts. It's so sweet of her!
examples.translation: Positive version — amazement and affection, not irritation.

sections.title: Forming the -ing form (spelling rules)
sections.content: Most verbs: just add -ing (talk→talking, eat→eating, sleep→sleeping, read→reading). Verb ends in -e: remove the e, then add -ing (make→making, write→writing, come→coming, have→having, give→giving). Short verb (1 syllable) ending in ONE vowel + ONE consonant: double the consonant (run→running, sit→sitting, swim→swimming, stop→stopping, get→getting, put→putting). Exceptions: don't double -w, -x, -y (fix→fixing, snow→snowing, play→playing). Verb ends in -ie: change to -y+ing (die→dying, lie→lying, tie→tying).

examples.sentence: talk→talking | make→making | run→running | die→dying
examples.translation: Four different spelling patterns — each has a rule.

examples.sentence: write→writing (not 'writeing') | sit→sitting (not 'siting')
examples.translation: Common mistakes — follow the rules carefully.

examples.sentence: listen→listening (two syllables, stress on 1st, so no doubling)
examples.translation: Listen doesn't double because the stress is on 'LIS', not 'ten'.

sections.title: Am/Is/Are + -ing
sections.content: I → am (I'm) / He, She, It → is (He's, She's, It's) / You, We, They → are (You're, We're, They're). Negative: am not (I'm not) / isn't / aren't + verb-ing. Question: Am/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?

examples.sentence: I'm working late tonight. / He's working late tonight.
examples.translation: Both correct — am for I, is for he.

examples.sentence: She isn't listening. / They aren't listening.
examples.translation: Negative: isn't for she, aren't for they.

examples.sentence: Are you enjoying the party? / Is she enjoying the party?
examples.translation: Question form — auxiliary verb comes first.

sections.title: Stative verbs — NEVER use these in Continuous
sections.content: Some verbs describe STATES (not actions) — they exist, they don't happen. You can't 'be in the middle of knowing something'. These verbs almost never appear in any Continuous tense. Categories: Mental states (know, understand, believe, think*, remember, forget, mean, realise). Emotions (love, hate, like, prefer, want, need, wish). Senses (see*, hear*, smell*, taste*, feel*). Possession (have*, own, belong, possess, contain). Appearance (seem, appear, look*, sound). *Some of these have action meanings where Continuous IS possible.

examples.sentence: I know the answer. ✓ / I am knowing the answer. ✗
examples.translation: Know is a stative verb — it exists, it doesn't happen.

examples.sentence: She wants a new laptop badly. ✓ / She is wanting a laptop. ✗
examples.translation: Want = state of desire — can't be in progress.

examples.sentence: I think he's wrong. ✓ (opinion) / I'm thinking about the problem. ✓ (mental action)
examples.translation: 'Think' has two meanings! Opinion = stative, Mental process = action.

examples.sentence: I have a car. ✓ (possession) / I'm having lunch. ✓ (experience/activity)
examples.translation: 'Have' has two meanings! Own = stative, Experience = action.

comparisons.explanation: The golden rule: Simple = GENERAL, ALWAYS, PERMANENTLY. Continuous = NOW, TEMPORARILY, IN PROGRESS. When you describe your life and habits, Simple. When you describe what's happening this moment or this week, Continuous. Watch out for stative verbs — they stay in Simple even when the situation is 'right now'.

examples.sentence: I read a lot. (Simple) — general habit, I'm a reader
examples.translation: This is who I am in general.

examples.sentence: I'm reading an amazing thriller right now. (Continuous) — specific book, in progress
examples.translation: This specific action is happening now — I'm halfway through it.

examples.sentence: He plays piano. (Simple) — he has this skill, this is who he is
examples.translation: A permanent ability and hobby.

examples.sentence: He's playing piano in the next room — can you hear it? (Continuous) — right now, you can hear it
examples.translation: The specific action is in progress as you speak.

commonMistakes: I am knowing the answer. ✗ → I know the answer. ✓ (know is stative)
She is having a car. ✗ → She has a car. ✓ (have = possession = stative)
What you are doing? ✗ → What are you doing? ✓ (auxiliary goes before subject in questions)
I am liking this film. ✗ → I like this film. ✓ (like is stative — states don't 'happen')
I am meeting her tomorrow — is this right? (YES ✓ — confirmed future arrangement)

grammarRules.summary: For completed actions in the past with a clear time frame. The most common past tense in everyday storytelling.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Past Simple puts a full stop in the past. The action is OVER. It started and ended in the past, and it has no direct connection to the present moment — it's history. Think of it as cutting the rope between past and present. The key markers: you can usually name WHEN it happened (yesterday, in 2015, last week, when I was a child). If someone asks 'When?', you have an answer — that's Past Simple territory. The moment something has a clear 'when', it belongs to the past.
grammarRules.structure: Subject + V2 (past form) | Subject + didn't + base verb | Did + subject + base verb?

usages.title: Completed actions at a specific time in the past
usages.explanation: Something happened and it's done — an hour ago, yesterday, last year, in 1969. There's always a specific time reference, either stated or clearly implied. The moment is over, the action is finished. This is the most common use of Past Simple — telling stories about what happened.

examples.sentence: I visited Japan last summer and absolutely loved it.
examples.translation: Last summer = specific time in the past. The visit is over.

examples.sentence: Neil Armstrong walked on the Moon in 1969.
examples.translation: 1969 = specific past time. Historical fact, completed event.

examples.sentence: She called me an hour ago but I missed it.
examples.translation: An hour ago = specific past moment. The call is over.

examples.sentence: We met at a conference in 2018 and stayed in touch ever since.
examples.translation: 2018 = the meeting is a completed past event with a specific time.

usages.title: A sequence of events in a story
usages.explanation: When you tell a story and describe events one after another, each action in Past Simple. The events are in order, each one completed before the next began. This is how you narrate — Past Simple is the backbone of all storytelling in English.

examples.sentence: I woke up, looked out the window, saw the snow, and immediately called in sick.
examples.translation: Four separate completed actions in sequence — each one done before the next.

examples.sentence: She opened the letter, read the first line, went pale, and sat down slowly.
examples.translation: A dramatic sequence — each action is a complete moment.

examples.sentence: He arrived at the airport, checked in, found his gate, and then realised he had the wrong passport.
examples.translation: Story told through a chain of completed Past Simple actions.

usages.title: Past habits and states that no longer exist
usages.explanation: Situations or habits that were true in the past but have now changed. Often used with when I was young/a child, in those days, in the 1990s. Note: 'used to' is also possible here and is slightly more emphatic about the contrast with now.

examples.sentence: When I was young, I played outside every single day until dark.
examples.translation: A past habit — I don't do this anymore. The situation has changed.

examples.sentence: My grandfather smoked a pipe and wore the same cardigan every day.
examples.translation: Past habits that were true during his lifetime.

examples.sentence: She lived in Paris for five years before moving to London.
examples.translation: A past state with a clear time frame — now she's in London.

sections.title: Regular verbs — adding -ed
sections.content: Most verbs: add -ed (work→worked, play→played, walk→walked). Verb ends in -e: add only -d (live→lived, love→loved, decide→decided). Verb ends in consonant+y: change y to i, add -ed (study→studied, try→tried, carry→carried, worry→worried). Short verb ending in one vowel+one consonant: double the consonant (stop→stopped, plan→planned, drop→dropped, prefer→preferred).

examples.sentence: I worked all weekend on that presentation.
examples.translation: Regular: work + ed = worked

examples.sentence: She studied medicine for six years.
examples.translation: consonant+y: study → studied (not 'studyed')

examples.sentence: He planned the trip carefully for months.
examples.translation: Short verb, 1 vowel + 1 consonant: plan → planned (double n)

sections.title: Irregular verbs — must be memorised
sections.content: These verbs have completely different past forms — no pattern, they must be learned. The most essential ones: go→went, come→came, see→saw, do→did, have→had, get→got, give→gave, take→took, make→made, know→knew, think→thought, say→said, tell→told, buy→bought, bring→brought, write→wrote, read→read (pronounced 'red'), meet→met, feel→felt, leave→left, find→found, lose→lost, run→ran, eat→ate, drink→drank, sleep→slept, speak→spoke, stand→stood, sit→sat, hear→heard, hold→held, keep→kept, send→sent, spend→spent, become→became, begin→began, break→broke, choose→chose, drive→drove, fall→fell, fly→flew, forget→forgot, grow→grew, know→knew, pay→paid, ride→rode, rise→rose, sell→sold, shake→shook, show→showed, sing→sang, steal→stole, swim→swam, teach→taught, understand→understood, wear→wore, win→won.

examples.sentence: I went to the doctor, he gave me some medicine, and I felt better by evening.
examples.translation: go→went, give→gave, feel→felt — three irregular past forms in one sentence.

examples.sentence: She bought a new laptop, took it home, and found it was broken.
examples.translation: buy→bought, take→took, find→found

examples.sentence: He thought about it all night but couldn't make a decision.
examples.translation: think→thought, make→made (in 'couldn't make')

sections.title: Negatives and questions — didn't and Did
sections.content: CRUCIAL: In negatives and questions, 'didn't/Did' takes ALL the past tense responsibility. The main verb returns to the BASE FORM — no -ed, no irregular form. This is the most common grammar mistake at A2 level. Negative: Subject + didn't (did not) + BASE VERB. Question: Did + subject + BASE VERB?

examples.sentence: I didn't go there. ✓ (NOT 'didn't went') / Did you go there? ✓ (NOT 'Did you went?')
examples.translation: After didn't/Did — always base verb. 'went' becomes 'go' again.

examples.sentence: She didn't enjoy the film at all.
examples.translation: Correct — 'didn't enjoy', not 'didn't enjoyed'

examples.sentence: Where did you buy that? — I bought it in Rome.
examples.translation: Question: Did + you + buy (base). Answer: I bought (irregular form back).

examples.sentence: Why didn't he tell us about the problem earlier?
examples.translation: didn't + tell (base form) — not 'didn't told'

comparisons.explanation: This is the most important distinction in English grammar — even native speakers of other languages struggle with it for years. RULE 1: If you mention a specific past time (yesterday, in 2015, last week, 3 days ago, when I was young) — ALWAYS Past Simple, NEVER Present Perfect. RULE 2: If the time is 'in my life', 'ever', 'never', 'before', without a specific moment — Present Perfect. RULE 3: If the past action has a direct, visible result NOW — Present Perfect. Think: 'Am I telling WHEN? Or am I just saying it happened?'

examples.sentence: I visited Rome last year. (Simple) — I'm saying WHEN. The trip is over, in the past.
examples.translation: Last year = specific past time = Past Simple. No connection to now.

examples.sentence: I've visited Rome. (Perfect) — I'm sharing a life experience. The visit happened, I'm not saying when.
examples.translation: The important thing is the fact, not when. It's my experience.

examples.sentence: She lost her keys yesterday. (Simple) — she lost them yesterday, specific time.
examples.translation: Yesterday is the key word — forces Past Simple.

examples.sentence: She's lost her keys. (Perfect) — she lost them, and she STILL doesn't have them right now!
examples.translation: The loss has a present result: she's standing here unable to get in.

comparisons.explanation: Past Simple = a completed point action (short, done). Past Continuous = an action in progress (long, ongoing background). They work TOGETHER in stories: Continuous provides the scene/background, Simple interrupts it with an event. 'I was walking home (background scene) when it suddenly started raining (the event that interrupts).'

examples.sentence: I read the report. (Simple) — I finished it, start to finish, done.
examples.translation: The reading is complete.

examples.sentence: I was reading the report when my boss walked in. (Continuous+Simple)
examples.translation: Reading = background in progress. Walked in = sudden interruption.

signalWords: this morning (if the day is over)
commonMistakes: I didn't went there. ✗ → I didn't go there. ✓ (after didn't, always BASE verb)
She didn't liked the food. ✗ → She didn't like the food. ✓ (same rule)
Did you went? ✗ → Did you go? ✓ (Did already marks the past)
I have seen him yesterday. ✗ → I saw him yesterday. ✓ (yesterday forces Past Simple)
We go to Paris last summer. ✗ → We went to Paris last summer. ✓ (past event needs past form)

grammarRules.summary: Describes actions in progress at a specific moment in the past, background scenes, and interrupted past actions.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Past Continuous is like a security camera recording from the past. You zoom in on a specific moment in time, and you see that an action was ALREADY IN PROGRESS — it had started before that moment and hadn't finished yet. The key image: it's not a point, it's a line. You're looking at the MIDDLE of an action, not its beginning or end. Most commonly, Past Continuous paints the BACKGROUND SCENE of a story, while Past Simple delivers the events that happen against that background.

usages.title: Action in progress at a specific past moment
usages.explanation: You pick a specific time in the past (at 10pm, at that moment, this time last year) and ask: what was happening then? The action was already running, it wasn't finished yet. You're looking at the middle of it.

examples.sentence: At 3am last night, I was still writing the essay. My roommate came in and couldn't believe it.
examples.translation: At 3am = specific moment. The writing was in progress — I started before 3am, hadn't finished.

examples.sentence: This time last year, I was travelling through Southeast Asia on a shoestring budget.
examples.translation: This time last year = specific past moment. The travelling was in progress at that point.

examples.sentence: What were you doing at midnight when the neighbours called the police?
examples.translation: Asking what was in progress at that specific moment (midnight).

usages.title: Background action interrupted by a sudden event (when/while)
usages.explanation: The most common pattern: a long action (Continuous) was in progress, and a short event (Simple) suddenly happened in the middle of it. The Continuous action is the 'scene', the Simple action is the 'event'. 'While' usually introduces the background. 'When' introduces either the interruption or the moment.

examples.sentence: I was having a shower when the fire alarm went off.
examples.translation: Having a shower = background (long, in progress). Went off = sudden event that interrupted it.

examples.sentence: While she was sleeping, someone quietly broke into the flat.
examples.translation: Sleeping = background. Broke into = the event during that background.

examples.sentence: He was walking his dog in the park when he found a wallet full of cash.
examples.translation: Walking the dog = background scene. Found = the surprising event.

examples.sentence: When I arrived at the party, everyone was already dancing and laughing.
examples.translation: Arrived = my action (Simple). Was dancing/laughing = scene I walked into (Continuous).

usages.title: Two parallel actions happening simultaneously
usages.explanation: When two ongoing actions were happening at the SAME TIME in the past — both in Continuous, connected with 'while' or 'as'. Neither interrupts the other — they run in parallel.

examples.sentence: While I was cooking dinner, he was setting the table and talking on the phone.
examples.translation: Both actions in progress at the same time — running in parallel.

examples.sentence: She was reading in the garden while her children were playing noisily around her.
examples.translation: Two simultaneous ongoing actions — both backgrounds, no interruption.

examples.sentence: As the doctor was examining the patient, the nurse was preparing the injection.
examples.translation: Parallel actions — happening at the same time in the past.

usages.title: Describing a past scene or atmosphere
usages.explanation: In creative writing or storytelling, Past Continuous sets the scene — it describes what was happening around the main events, creating atmosphere and context. When you read a novel, the background descriptions are often in Past Continuous.

examples.sentence: The city was sleeping. Fog was rolling in from the river. Somewhere, a dog was barking.
examples.translation: Scene-setting — all background atmosphere, creating a mood.

examples.sentence: When I arrived at the old house, the windows were glowing warmly, smoke was rising from the chimney, and someone was playing violin inside.
examples.translation: Everything I saw = ongoing background situations creating the scene.

sections.title: Was vs Were — and the -ing form
sections.content: I / He / She / It → was + verb-ing. You / We / They → were + verb-ing. Negative: wasn't (was not) / weren't (were not) + verb-ing. Question: Was/Were + subject + verb-ing? The -ing form follows the same spelling rules as Present Continuous.

examples.sentence: I was sleeping. / We were sleeping.
examples.translation: was for I/he/she/it — were for you/we/they

examples.sentence: Was she crying when you found her? / Were they arguing again?
examples.translation: Question: Was/Were at the start.

examples.sentence: It wasn't raining when we left, but it was getting cloudy.
examples.translation: wasn't for it — the weather was changing (in progress).

comparisons.explanation: The perfect partnership. Past Simple = short, completed action (a DOT on the timeline). Past Continuous = long, in-progress action (a LINE on the timeline). In stories, Continuous sets the stage and Simple delivers the drama. The trigger words: 'when' often introduces Past Simple (the event), 'while' almost always introduces Past Continuous (the background).

examples.sentence: I watched a film last night. (Simple) — I watched it from beginning to end, it's done.
examples.translation: Complete, finished action.

examples.sentence: I was watching a film when my friend burst in with terrible news. (Continuous interrupted by Simple)
examples.translation: Film = background in progress. Burst in = sudden interrupting event.

commonMistakes: I was see a film. ✗ → I was watching a film. ✓ (needs -ing form: watch→watching)
While I cooked, she was eating. ✗ → While I was cooking, she was eating. ✓ (while = both in Continuous)
She were tired. ✗ → She was tired. ✓ (she → was, not were)
I was knowing — ✗ (know is stative — even in past, use 'I knew')
When I was a child, I was going to school. ✗ → I went to school. ✓ (regular past habit = Simple, not Continuous)

grammarRules.summary: The bridge between past and present. Used for life experiences, recent events with present relevance, and situations that started in the past and continue now.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Present Perfect is the most misunderstood tense for learners because it looks like the past but acts like the present. Here's the key insight: Present Perfect is NOT really about the past — it's about NOW. You mention something that happened in the past because it matters RIGHT NOW. Either you're sharing a life experience (the experience makes you who you are NOW), or something just happened and it has an effect NOW, or a situation started in the past and is STILL true NOW. The moment you give a specific past time (yesterday, in 2010, last week), the connection to the present disappears and you need Past Simple.

usages.title: Life experiences (ever / never)
usages.explanation: You're talking about whether something happened at any point in someone's life — you're not asking WHEN, just WHETHER. This is like checking items off a life bucket list. 'Have you ever climbed a mountain?' doesn't care when — it cares whether you have that experience at all. 'Never' means the experience hasn't happened yet in your entire life so far.

examples.sentence: Have you ever tried whale watching? It's absolutely incredible.
examples.translation: Ever = at any time in your life. Not asking when — asking IF.

examples.sentence: I've never been to a country where I don't speak the language. It scares me.
examples.translation: Never = zero times in my entire life. The experience is absent.

examples.sentence: She's ridden an elephant, jumped out of a plane, and dived with sharks.
examples.translation: Three life experiences — they're part of who she is, her story.

examples.sentence: This is the first time I've ever felt truly at home somewhere.
examples.translation: 'This is the first time' always triggers Present Perfect.

usages.title: Recent events with a present result (just)
usages.explanation: Something happened very recently — moments, minutes, or hours ago — and the result or effect is visible or relevant RIGHT NOW. 'Just' almost always goes between have/has and the past participle. The key: the past event explains the present situation.

examples.sentence: I can't come out tonight — I've just twisted my ankle playing football.
examples.translation: Just twisted = happened recently. The result: I'm in pain NOW, can't walk.

examples.sentence: The meeting has just finished — everyone is leaving the room.
examples.translation: Just finished = moments ago. The result: the room is emptying NOW.

examples.sentence: Great news — she's just been offered her dream job!
examples.translation: Just offered = happened recently. Present state: she's celebrating NOW.

examples.sentence: Don't buy bread — I've just bought some on my way home.
examples.translation: Just bought = recent past. Result: we have bread NOW, no need to buy more.

usages.title: Already and Yet
usages.explanation: ALREADY = done sooner than expected (in positive statements, sometimes questions). It goes between have/has and the past participle, or at the end. YET = by now, by this point (in negatives and questions only). It usually goes at the end of the sentence. Both words contain the idea of 'by this point in time' — they link past action to present expectation.

examples.sentence: I've already finished — I worked through lunch.
examples.translation: Already = done before expected. I'm finished NOW, ahead of schedule.

examples.sentence: Have you already eaten? I was going to cook!
examples.translation: Already in a question = surprised it happened so soon.

examples.sentence: I haven't finished yet. I need another hour.
examples.translation: Yet in negative = not done by this point, but it's expected.

examples.sentence: Has the post arrived yet? — Not yet, unfortunately.
examples.translation: Yet in question = has it happened by now? Not yet = still hasn't happened.

usages.title: Situations continuing from past to present (for/since)
usages.explanation: A situation STARTED in the past and IS STILL TRUE NOW. This is where for and since become essential. FOR + a period of time (how long the situation has lasted: for 3 years, for ages, for a week). SINCE + a specific starting point (when the situation began: since Monday, since 2015, since I was a child). The crucial difference from Past Simple: the situation HASN'T ENDED. If you use Past Simple, it means it's over.

examples.sentence: I've lived in this apartment for six years, and I love it more every day.
examples.translation: FOR six years = the duration. I STILL live there — not past, ongoing.

examples.sentence: She's worked at the same company since graduating in 2014.
examples.translation: SINCE 2014 = starting point. She STILL works there.

examples.sentence: They've been married for 42 years. Still going strong.
examples.translation: FOR 42 years = duration of an ongoing state.

examples.sentence: I haven't eaten anything since this morning and I'm starving.
examples.translation: SINCE this morning = when I last ate. I'm still in this state (hungry) NOW.

sections.title: Past Participle (V3) — regular and irregular
sections.content: Regular verbs: V3 = base+ed (worked, played, studied, stopped — same as Past Simple). Irregular verbs (different from both base and Past Simple — must memorise): go→gone, come→come, see→seen, do→done, have→had, get→got/gotten, give→given, take→taken, make→made, know→known, think→thought, say→said, buy→bought, write→written, read→read, meet→met, feel→felt, speak→spoken, eat→eaten, drink→drunk, find→found, lose→lost, break→broken, choose→chosen, fall→fallen, fly→flown, forget→forgotten, grow→grown, hide→hidden, hold→held, keep→kept, leave→left, pay→paid, prove→proven, ride→ridden, rise→risen, run→run, sell→sold, send→sent, show→shown, sing→sung, sleep→slept, spend→spent, stand→stood, steal→stolen, swim→swum, teach→taught, tear→torn, tell→told, throw→thrown, understand→understood, wake→woken, wear→worn, win→won.

examples.sentence: I've lived here for years. (lived — regular, same as past simple)
examples.translation: Regular: live → lived → lived (V1/V2/V3 all follow the rule)

examples.sentence: She's never spoken to him directly. (spoken — different from 'spoke'!)
examples.translation: Speak → spoke → spoken. V2 and V3 are DIFFERENT — common mistake!

examples.sentence: Have you seen the new series? It's unbelievable. (seen — not 'saw'!)
examples.translation: See → saw → seen. Use 'seen' with Present Perfect, not 'saw'.

sections.title: Have vs Has — and contractions
sections.content: I/You/We/They → have (I've, You've, We've, They've). He/She/It → has (He's, She's, It's). Negative: haven't / hasn't. Question: Have you...? / Has she...? Be careful: He's can mean 'He is' OR 'He has' — context tells you which.

examples.sentence: I've already done it. / She's already done it.
examples.translation: I've = I have | She's = She has (in this context — not 'She is')

examples.sentence: They haven't arrived yet. / He hasn't arrived yet.
examples.translation: haven't for they/we/you/I | hasn't for he/she/it

examples.sentence: Have you ever tried surfing? / Has he ever tried surfing?
examples.translation: Have for you/I/we/they | Has for he/she/it in questions

comparisons.explanation: The critical distinction. DECISION RULE: Does your sentence contain a specific past time expression (yesterday, last week, in 2005, ago, this morning if it's evening now, when I was young)? → Past Simple ONLY, no exceptions. Does your sentence contain ever, never, just, already, yet, for, since, recently, lately? → Present Perfect ONLY. No specific time = Present Perfect. Specific time = Past Simple. You cannot mix them in the same clause.

examples.sentence: I have visited Paris. (Perfect) — my life experience, no specific time mentioned
examples.translation: It's a fact about me — I carry this experience with me.

examples.sentence: I visited Paris in 2019 and stayed for a week. (Simple) — specific time given
examples.translation: In 2019 forces Past Simple — the trip is in the past, finished.

examples.sentence: Have you eaten? (Perfect) — are you hungry right now?
examples.translation: Relevant to NOW — affects whether we should cook.

examples.sentence: Did you eat at that place? (Simple) — asking about a specific occasion
examples.translation: About a specific event — were you there, at that restaurant, then?

commonMistakes: I have seen him yesterday. ✗ → I saw him yesterday. ✓ (yesterday = specific time = Past Simple)
Did you ever try sushi? ✗ → Have you ever tried sushi? ✓ (life experience = Present Perfect)
She has went there. ✗ → She has gone there. ✓ (go → went → GONE — use past participle, not past simple)
I live here since 2010. ✗ → I have lived here since 2010. ✓ (since = Present Perfect)
I've bought a car last year. ✗ → I bought a car last year. ✓ (last year = specific time = Past Simple)

grammarRules.summary: Emphasises the DURATION of an ongoing activity from the past up to now, and visible present results of recent activity.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Present Perfect Continuous is all about showing that an activity has been ONGOING — not just that it happened, but that it has been in PROGRESS for a period of time leading up to this moment. There are two main feelings it conveys: 1) This has been happening for a while and is still happening (or just stopped). 2) Someone was doing this for a while and you can see the evidence right now. Where Present Perfect Simple asks 'Has it happened?', Present Perfect Continuous asks 'How long has it been happening?'

usages.title: Activity has been in progress up to now (still ongoing or just stopped)
usages.explanation: The activity started in the past and has been continuously happening up until this moment — it may still be ongoing or have just stopped. FOR and SINCE are your most important signal words here. The emphasis is on DURATION — how long the process has been running.

examples.sentence: I've been learning Spanish for two years and I'm finally having real conversations.
examples.translation: Two years = the duration of the ongoing process. I'm still learning — it hasn't stopped.

examples.sentence: She's been working on this novel since 2021. It's almost finished now.
examples.translation: Since 2021 = starting point. She's been continuously working on it all this time.

examples.sentence: They've been living together for six months and are talking about marriage.
examples.translation: FOR six months = how long. Still living together, the situation is ongoing.

examples.sentence: How long have you been waiting? — About 40 minutes. I'm furious.
examples.translation: How long = asking about duration of ongoing activity.

usages.title: Visible present result of a recent ongoing activity
usages.explanation: You can see the evidence of what someone has been doing. The activity may be finished now, but its effects are clear in the present. This is a key difference from Present Perfect Simple: Simple states a fact; Continuous explains a visible condition.

examples.sentence: You've been crying, haven't you? Your eyes are all red.
examples.translation: I can see the evidence NOW (red eyes) — the crying explains the current state.

examples.sentence: The kitchen smells amazing — have you been baking?
examples.translation: I can smell the result NOW. The baking explains the current smell.

examples.sentence: He's exhausted because he's been training twice a day for the competition.
examples.translation: Exhaustion is the visible result of the ongoing training activity.

examples.sentence: Why are your clothes all muddy? — I've been gardening all afternoon.
examples.translation: Muddy clothes = visible evidence. The activity explains the current appearance.

sections.title: Form: have/has + been + -ing
sections.content: I/You/We/They have been + verb-ing (I've been working). He/She/It has been + verb-ing (She's been waiting). Negative: haven't/hasn't been + verb-ing. Question: Have/Has + subject + been + verb-ing? The -ing spelling rules are the same as always.

examples.sentence: I've been thinking about your proposal all day.
examples.translation: have been thinking — ongoing mental activity throughout the day.

examples.sentence: Has it been raining long? The streets are flooded.
examples.translation: Question about duration of ongoing past activity.

examples.sentence: They haven't been communicating well lately — there's clearly tension.
examples.translation: Negative — the lack of communication has been an ongoing situation.

comparisons.explanation: Simple = completed result / how many times / fact. Continuous = duration / ongoing process / visible evidence. The classic test: 'I've written 3 emails' (Simple — result, how many, completed). 'I've been writing emails all morning' (Continuous — the activity was in progress for a long time). Some verbs (stative verbs: know, love, want, believe, own) DON'T appear in Continuous even in this tense — 'I've known her for years' NOT 'I've been knowing her'.

examples.sentence: I've read 50 pages. (Simple) — result: 50 pages are done, completed.
examples.translation: Focus on achievement/result — 50 pages.

examples.sentence: I've been reading for three hours. (Continuous) — process: three hours of ongoing activity.
examples.translation: Focus on duration — three hours of effort.

examples.sentence: She's painted the bedroom. (Simple) — result: the bedroom is now painted.
examples.translation: The bedroom is done. Result matters.

examples.sentence: She's been painting all day — she looks exhausted. (Continuous) — activity in progress all day
examples.translation: The effort and duration matter, not whether it's finished.

commonMistakes: I have been knowing her for years. ✗ → I have known her for years. ✓ (know is stative — no Continuous)
I've been here since two hours. ✗ → I've been here for two hours. ✓ (FOR + period, not since)
She has been worked here since 2018. ✗ → She has been working here since 2018. ✓ (needs -ing form)
How long are you waiting? ✗ → How long have you been waiting? ✓ (duration from past to now = Perfect Continuous)

grammarRules.summary: The 'past of the past' — used when you need to show that one past action happened BEFORE another past action.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Imagine you're already in the past, telling a story in Past Simple. Then you need to refer to something that happened even EARLIER — before the story started. That earlier event gets Past Perfect. It's like going one layer deeper into the past. Past Simple = the story. Past Perfect = the backstory. The key concept is SEQUENCE: which of two past events happened first? The earlier one gets Past Perfect (had + past participle). This tense exists to prevent confusion about the order of events.

usages.title: One past action happened before another past action
usages.explanation: You're telling a story in the past (Past Simple), and you want to mention something that happened earlier — before the story began. Past Perfect clarifies the timeline. Without it, listeners might think all events happened in order, which could be wrong or confusing.

examples.sentence: When I arrived at the cinema, the film had already started — I missed the opening.
examples.translation: The film starting happened BEFORE I arrived. Two past events, film start = earlier = Past Perfect.

examples.sentence: She looked completely different. She had cut her hair and dyed it blonde.
examples.translation: Cutting and dyeing happened before I saw her. The change explains her current appearance.

examples.sentence: He couldn't get into his flat because he had forgotten his keys at the office.
examples.translation: Forgetting happened first. Not being able to get in happened second. Clear sequence.

examples.sentence: By the time the ambulance arrived, the patient had already regained consciousness.
examples.translation: Regaining consciousness = EARLIER. Ambulance arriving = LATER. By the time = typical Past Perfect trigger.

usages.title: Reporting past speech and thoughts
usages.explanation: In reported speech, if you're already in the past tense and reporting what someone thought or said, you often backshift further into Past Perfect to show the original thought was even earlier.

examples.sentence: She realised she had made a terrible mistake.
examples.translation: Realised = past. Made the mistake = even earlier past (before the realisation).

examples.sentence: He said he had never seen anything so beautiful in his life.
examples.translation: Said = past. Had never seen = up to that point in his past life.

examples.sentence: I suddenly remembered that I had promised to call her.
examples.translation: Remembered = past. Promising happened even earlier — it was an earlier commitment.

usages.title: Third Conditional (if + Past Perfect)
usages.explanation: Past Perfect is essential in Third Conditional — when you imagine how the past could have been different. 'If + Past Perfect, would have + past participle.' You're looking back at a past situation and imagining a different outcome.

examples.sentence: If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam.
examples.translation: I didn't study hard (past fact). Past Perfect = the imaginary different past.

examples.sentence: She wouldn't have been late if she hadn't stopped for coffee.
examples.translation: She did stop for coffee (reality). Perfect = the imaginary version of the past.

sections.title: Form: had + past participle for ALL subjects
sections.content: I/You/He/She/It/We/They → had + V3. The same for everyone — no had/has split. Contractions: I'd, you'd, he'd, she'd, it'd, we'd, they'd (note: these same contractions are also used for would — context tells you which). Negative: hadn't (had not) + V3. Question: Had + subject + V3?

examples.sentence: I had finished / She had finished / They had finished — all the same form
examples.translation: Had is constant for all subjects — no exception.

examples.sentence: He hadn't eaten since the previous day. He was starving.
examples.translation: Negative past perfect — the not-eating continued up to a past point.

examples.sentence: Had you met before, or was it the first time?
examples.translation: Question about a prior past event — met before the 'first time' scenario.

comparisons.explanation: Past Simple = both events in the past, you're telling them in order (first this, then that). Past Perfect = you need to JUMP BACK to show something happened even BEFORE the already-past moment. If context makes the order clear without Past Perfect (using 'first', 'then', 'after'), Past Perfect is optional. If the order could be confused, Past Perfect is necessary.

examples.sentence: He had lunch and then he left. (Simple, order is obvious with 'then')
examples.translation: Order clear from 'then' — Past Perfect not necessary here.

examples.sentence: When I got there, she left. (Simple — she left AFTER I arrived, simultaneous-ish)
examples.translation: She left AFTER I got there — two sequential past events.

examples.sentence: When I got there, she had left. (Perfect — she left BEFORE I arrived)
examples.translation: She was gone by the time I arrived — past perfect shows 'already gone'.

commonMistakes: When I arrived, she has left. ✗ → When I arrived, she had left. ✓ (has = present perfect, not past perfect)
I had went there. ✗ → I had gone there. ✓ (always use V3 past participle, not V2)
She had finished before I arrived. — Is this right? YES ✓ (finished before arriving = correct use)
By the time he called, I was going to bed. ✗ → I had gone to bed. ✓ (by the time = Past Perfect)

grammarRules.summary: Shows that an activity had been ongoing for a period of time before a specific past moment — the 'was in progress before this' tense.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Past Perfect Continuous is the deepest layer of the past. You're already in the past (your story is set there), and you need to show that BEFORE a certain past moment, an activity had been running continuously. It combines the 'before the past' feel of Past Perfect with the 'in progress, duration' feel of Continuous. Think of it as: 'Up until that past moment, this had been going on for a while — and here is how long.' It often explains WHY something was the way it was at a past moment.

usages.title: Activity in progress for a period before a past moment
usages.explanation: Something had been happening continuously up until a specific point in the past. You're explaining the background — the duration of an activity that preceded the main past event. FOR and SINCE often appear here, measuring how long the activity had been going on.

examples.sentence: By the time the rescue team found them, they had been surviving in the mountains for 11 days.
examples.translation: Found = past event. Had been surviving for 11 days = the ongoing activity that preceded it.

examples.sentence: She had been waiting at the airport for three hours when her flight was finally cancelled.
examples.translation: Waiting = ongoing activity. Three hours = duration. Flight cancelled = past event that ended the waiting.

examples.sentence: When the doctor examined him, it was clear he had been drinking heavily for years.
examples.translation: The examination revealed evidence of a long-running past habit.

usages.title: Explaining the cause of a past state
usages.explanation: A past activity explains why something was the way it was at a specific past moment. The activity is the CAUSE, the past state is the EFFECT. This is parallel to how Present Perfect Continuous explains current states.

examples.sentence: Her eyes were red because she had been crying for hours.
examples.translation: Red eyes = the past state. Crying = the activity that caused it. Had been = duration.

examples.sentence: He was completely exhausted — he had been driving non-stop for eighteen hours.
examples.translation: Exhausted = the past state I observed. Driving = the cause, with duration.

examples.sentence: The children were filthy when they came inside — they had been playing in the mud.
examples.translation: Filthy = past state. Playing in the mud = ongoing activity that caused it.

sections.title: Form: had + been + -ing (same for all subjects)
sections.content: I/You/He/She/It/We/They → had been + verb-ing. Contractions: I'd been working, she'd been waiting. Negative: hadn't been + verb-ing. Question: Had + subject + been + verb-ing?

examples.sentence: I'd been working on the project for weeks before I finally cracked it.
examples.translation: Weeks of continuous effort before the breakthrough past moment.

examples.sentence: Hadn't they been living together before the wedding?
examples.translation: Question about a past ongoing situation that preceded another past event.

comparisons.explanation: Past Perfect Simple = completed action before a past moment (focus on result or completion). Past Perfect Continuous = ongoing activity before a past moment (focus on DURATION and PROCESS). Often you can use either, but Continuous emphasises 'how long' and 'in the middle of an activity'.

examples.sentence: He had written three reports by noon. (Simple) — result: three reports exist, done.
examples.translation: Focus: three reports completed. The number/result matters.

examples.sentence: He had been writing reports all morning when the system crashed. (Continuous) — activity in progress
examples.translation: Focus: the ongoing activity, the effort. The system crash interrupted the process.

commonMistakes: She had been knew him for years. ✗ → She had known him for years. ✓ (know is stative — no Continuous)
They had been work there. ✗ → They had been working there. ✓ (needs -ing form)
He had been waited for an hour when she arrived. ✗ → had been waiting ✓ (-ing, not past simple)

grammarRules.summary: Used for spontaneous decisions, predictions, promises, offers, and facts about the future.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Will is the most flexible future tool in English. Unlike 'going to' (which needs a plan already in place) and Present Continuous (which needs a fixed arrangement), 'will' works in the moment. There are two core feelings for 'will': First, spontaneity — you decide RIGHT NOW as you're speaking, not before. Second, prediction without evidence — you THINK something will happen based on your knowledge, experience, or opinion, but there's no physical sign of it right now. Will is also the polite future for promises, offers, and requests.
grammarRules.structure: will + base verb (all subjects) | won't + base verb | Will + subject + base verb?

usages.title: Spontaneous decisions — made right now, not planned before
usages.explanation: You had no plan. In the moment of speaking, you decide. This is the critical difference from 'going to' — Going to = you thought about it earlier. Will = you just decided. If you see a situation and immediately react, that's Will. Classic scenario: someone says they need help → you respond with 'I'll help you' (you just decided this instant).

examples.sentence: — The phone's ringing! — I'll get it! (decided in this moment)
examples.translation: No prior plan to answer — the situation arose, you reacted with Will.

examples.sentence: You look absolutely exhausted. I'll make you a cup of tea.
examples.translation: You didn't plan this tea — you saw the situation and decided now.

examples.sentence: — Oh no, I forgot my wallet. — Don't worry, I'll pay for lunch.
examples.translation: No plan existed — the situation demanded a spontaneous decision.

examples.sentence: That bag looks heavy. Here, I'll carry it for you.
examples.translation: Spontaneous offer — saw the problem, immediately offered help.

usages.title: Predictions based on opinion or knowledge (no present evidence)
usages.explanation: You THINK something will happen — based on general knowledge, experience, gut feeling. The key difference from 'going to predictions': Going to = you can SEE evidence right now (dark clouds = it's going to rain). Will = there's nothing visible right now, just your informed opinion or belief. Often introduced by: I think, I believe, I expect, I'm sure, I doubt, probably.

examples.sentence: I think you'll love this restaurant — the food is incredible.
examples.translation: Your opinion/prediction — no evidence visible now, just knowledge.

examples.sentence: Scientists predict that the population will reach 10 billion by 2050.
examples.translation: Data-based prediction about the distant future — not visible evidence now.

examples.sentence: I doubt she'll agree to that — she's too stubborn.
examples.translation: I doubt = negative prediction based on knowledge of her character.

examples.sentence: Don't worry — everything will be fine in the end. It always is.
examples.translation: Reassurance based on general experience and optimism, not visible evidence.

usages.title: Promises, offers, requests, and threats
usages.explanation: Will is the language of commitment and politeness. When you promise, you commit with will. When you threaten, you use will. When you offer, you use will. When you ask someone to do something (request), you use will. All of these are spoken decisions and commitments happening in the moment.

examples.sentence: I promise I'll call you the moment I land. I won't forget.
examples.translation: Promise = commitment made now. Won't forget = promised not to.

examples.sentence: Will you help me move these boxes? They're really heavy.
examples.translation: Request — you're asking for a decision from the other person.

examples.sentence: If you say that one more time, I'll leave and never come back.
examples.translation: Threat — will is used to express the consequence of future behaviour.

examples.sentence: Shall I open the window? — Yes, please, it's boiling in here.
examples.translation: Offer with Shall I (more formal). Will also works: I'll open it.

usages.title: Future facts and certainties
usages.explanation: Some future things are simply going to happen — established facts about the future. Not a plan, not a prediction — just something that will definitely occur.

examples.sentence: The sun will rise at 6:23am tomorrow.
examples.translation: Future fact — this will happen regardless of anyone's plans.

examples.sentence: She'll be 30 next April.
examples.translation: Future fact — birthdays are facts, not plans.

examples.sentence: The contract will expire on the 31st of December.
examples.translation: Legal fact about the future — not a prediction, not a plan.

sections.title: Form — will for all subjects, contractions
sections.content: Will stays the same for all subjects — I will, you will, he will, she will, it will, we will, they will. Contractions: I'll, you'll, he'll, she'll, it'll, we'll, they'll. Negative: will not = won't (I won't, she won't). Question: Will + subject + base verb? The main verb is ALWAYS in base form after will.

examples.sentence: I'll see you tomorrow. / He'll call you back.
examples.translation: Contractions in natural speech — almost always used in conversation.

examples.sentence: She won't be happy about this decision at all.
examples.translation: won't = will not — strong negative future.

examples.sentence: Will you be at the meeting on Friday?
examples.translation: Simple question about future situation.

comparisons.explanation: This is one of the most important distinctions in English. WILL = decided NOW, in this moment (spontaneous) OR prediction based on opinion. GOING TO = decided BEFORE (plan/intention exists) OR prediction based on visible current evidence. Ask yourself: 'Did I plan this earlier, or am I deciding right now?' Earlier plan = Going to. Right now = Will.

examples.sentence: — What are you doing tonight? — I'm going to study. (Going to — I planned this before you asked)
examples.translation: Plan existed before the conversation.

examples.sentence: — What do you want to do? — I don't know... I'll study, I suppose. (Will — deciding now)
examples.translation: No prior plan — deciding in this moment.

examples.sentence: It's going to rain — look at those clouds! (Going to — visible evidence: clouds)
examples.translation: You can SEE the evidence → Going to.

examples.sentence: Take an umbrella — I think it'll rain later. (Will — opinion, no visible evidence now)
examples.translation: Your general knowledge/feeling, not something you can see.

commonMistakes: I will to call you. ✗ → I will call you. ✓ (no 'to' after will)
I'll call him tomorrow — is this planned or spontaneous? (Both are fine! Context determines, not grammar)
She wills come. ✗ → She will come. ✓ (will never changes form)
Will you like it? ✗ (asking about a state) → Do you like it? ✓ (likes are states, use Simple)
I think she will come. ✓ vs I think she comes. ✗ (future prediction needs will)

grammarRules.summary: Describes actions that will be in progress at a specific moment in the future, or politely asks about someone's plans.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Future Continuous does for the future what Past Continuous does for the past — it puts you in the MIDDLE of an action at a specific time. Instead of asking 'Will it happen?', it says 'At this particular future moment, the action will be in full progress — already started, not yet finished.' Imagine setting an alarm for a time in the future and checking: 'What will I be doing when that alarm goes off?' The answer is Future Continuous. It's also brilliantly useful for politely enquiring about plans without imposing.

usages.title: Action in progress at a specific future moment
usages.explanation: You pick a moment in the future and zoom in: the action will already be running and won't be finished yet. It's the line-not-a-point idea, projected into the future. Common with: at this time tomorrow, at 9pm tonight, when you arrive, this time next week.

examples.sentence: At this time tomorrow, I'll be sitting on a beach in the Maldives. Can't wait.
examples.translation: Tomorrow at this time = specific future moment. Sitting on a beach = in progress at that moment.

examples.sentence: Don't call between 2 and 4pm — I'll be in a meeting with the board.
examples.translation: Between 2 and 4 = the period. In a meeting = in progress throughout that time.

examples.sentence: When you arrive at the airport, look for me — I'll be wearing a red jacket and carrying a sign.
examples.translation: When you arrive = future moment. Wearing and carrying = in progress at that moment.

examples.sentence: This time next year, we'll be celebrating our first anniversary in Italy.
examples.translation: This time next year = specific future moment. Celebrating = in progress at that point.

usages.title: Polite and indirect enquiry about plans
usages.explanation: Future Continuous is softer and less imposing than 'Will you...?' or 'Are you going to...?' Using Future Continuous to ask about plans implies you're just enquiring about what was already planned — not pressuring someone to make a decision or do something for you. It's a mark of social intelligence and politeness.

examples.sentence: Will you be using the car later? I need to go to the supermarket.
examples.translation: Softer than 'Can I use the car?' — you're asking about their plan, not making a demand.

examples.sentence: Will you be seeing John today? Could you pass on a message for me?
examples.translation: Enquiring about their schedule gently — not asking them to arrange a meeting.

examples.sentence: Will you be attending the conference next week?
examples.translation: Polite professional enquiry — 'Are you coming?' feels more confrontational.

usages.title: Action that will happen as a matter of course (no special arrangement)
usages.explanation: Sometimes Future Continuous describes something that will naturally happen — not because of any special plan, but just as part of the normal flow of events. There's no specific decision — it'll just happen.

examples.sentence: I'll be flying over the Alps on my way to Rome — I'll try to get a window seat.
examples.translation: The flying over Alps is just part of the journey — no special plan needed, it'll happen naturally.

examples.sentence: The construction will be continuing for another 18 months, I'm afraid.
examples.translation: It'll be in progress — it's the natural continuation, no new decision required.

sections.title: Form: will + be + verb-ing
sections.content: Will be + verb-ing for all subjects. Contractions: I'll be working, she'll be waiting. Negative: won't be + verb-ing. Question: Will + subject + be + verb-ing?

examples.sentence: I'll be working from home all day tomorrow.
examples.translation: will be working — in progress all of tomorrow (from home specifically)

examples.sentence: She won't be attending the party — she has other plans.
examples.translation: won't be attending — won't be in progress at that time

examples.sentence: Will he be joining us for dinner tonight?
examples.translation: Polite enquiry about plans — Will + be + joining

comparisons.explanation: Will = simple future fact or decision (a DOT in the future). Will be + -ing = an action in progress at a future moment (a LINE in the future). 'I'll call you at 8' = I'll make the call at 8 (a point). 'I'll be calling clients between 8 and 11' = the calling will be in progress during that period.

examples.sentence: I'll write the report tomorrow. (Will) — I'll complete the report tomorrow.
examples.translation: The whole action will happen — focus on completion.

examples.sentence: I'll be writing the report all day tomorrow. (Continuous) — the process will be running all day.
examples.translation: The action will be in progress — focus on the duration.

commonMistakes: I will be work there. ✗ → I will be working there. ✓ (needs -ing form)
At 9pm, I'll be watch TV. ✗ → I'll be watching TV. ✓
Will you be go to the shop? ✗ → Will you be going to the shop? ✓ (-ing after 'be')

grammarRules.summary: Used to say that an action will be COMPLETED before a specific point in the future.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Future Perfect travels into the future and then looks BACK at what will have been completed by then. You pick a future deadline or moment, and you're saying: 'Before that moment arrives, this action will be done and finished.' The critical word is BY — 'by the time', 'by Friday', 'by next year', 'by the time you read this'. BY signals Future Perfect almost every time. It's about COMPLETION BEFORE A FUTURE DEADLINE.

usages.title: Action completed before a future deadline
usages.explanation: You set a future deadline and say that by that time, the action will be finished. The action is either already in progress, or you're confident it will be completed in time. 'By' is almost always present — it's the defining feature of this tense.

examples.sentence: By the time you wake up, I will have already left for the airport.
examples.translation: By the time you wake up = the future deadline. Left = will be done before that moment.

examples.sentence: By December, she will have been at the company for exactly ten years.
examples.translation: By December = deadline. Ten years = the duration that will be complete.

examples.sentence: Don't call me before 8pm — I won't have finished the surgery by then.
examples.translation: Before 8pm = the deadline. Surgery won't be complete = it'll still be in progress.

examples.sentence: By the time this film ends, we will have been waiting for over three hours.
examples.translation: Film ending = future deadline. Three hours of waiting = will be complete by then.

usages.title: Achievement and milestones
usages.explanation: Future Perfect is often used to talk about reaching milestones or accumulating experience by a future point. It gives a sense of accomplishment — projecting forward to a future moment of achievement.

examples.sentence: By the time he graduates, he will have spent seven years at that university.
examples.translation: Graduation = future milestone. Seven years = the achievement that will be complete.

examples.sentence: By 2030, scientists hope they will have found a cure for Alzheimer's.
examples.translation: 2030 = future deadline. Finding a cure = the hoped-for completion.

sections.title: Form: will + have + V3
sections.content: Will have + past participle for all subjects. Contractions: I'll have done, she'll have finished. Negative: won't have + V3. Question: Will + subject + have + V3? The past participle (V3) is the same as in Present Perfect and Past Perfect.

examples.sentence: By Friday, I'll have written all five reports.
examples.translation: By Friday = deadline. Written = past participle. All five = completed.

examples.sentence: She won't have returned from her trip by the time you need her.
examples.translation: She won't be back in time for your deadline.

examples.sentence: Will they have fixed the road by summer?
examples.translation: Summer = future deadline. Fixed = asking if it'll be complete by then.

comparisons.explanation: Future Simple = the action will happen at some point in the future (no deadline implied). Future Perfect = the action will be COMPLETED BEFORE a specific future deadline. The addition of 'by' changes everything.

examples.sentence: I'll finish the project next week. (Simple) — it'll get done next week
examples.translation: It happens next week — no specific deadline pressure.

examples.sentence: I'll have finished the project by next Friday. (Perfect) — done before Friday's deadline
examples.translation: By Friday = the deadline it must be completed before.

commonMistakes: By Friday, I will finish it. (Simple) vs By Friday, I will have finished it. (Perfect — more precise about completion before deadline)
I will have finish it. ✗ → I will have finished it. ✓ (needs past participle, not base verb)
By the time she arrives, I went. ✗ → I will have gone. ✓ (future context needs future tense)

grammarRules.summary: Shows how long an activity will have been in progress by a future point in time — emphasising duration up to a future moment.
grammarRules.coreConcept: Future Perfect Continuous is the most advanced tense — it combines Future Perfect's 'before a deadline' feeling with Continuous's 'ongoing process and duration' feeling. You look forward to a future moment and say: 'By that point, this activity will have been running continuously for X amount of time.' It always answers the question 'How long will it have been happening?' by a future deadline. Like all Perfect Continuous tenses, it emphasises EFFORT, PROCESS, and DURATION rather than completion or result.

usages.title: Duration of activity up to a future deadline
usages.explanation: By a specific future moment, an ongoing activity will have been in progress for a certain period. The emphasis is on how long the process will have been running — not whether it will be finished.

examples.sentence: By next March, I will have been learning English for exactly five years.
examples.translation: Next March = future deadline. Five years = how long the learning will have been going on.

examples.sentence: By the end of this project, we will have been working together for three years.
examples.translation: End of project = future deadline. Three years = the duration of collaboration.

examples.sentence: When she crosses the finish line, she will have been running for over four hours.
examples.translation: Crossing the finish line = future moment. Four hours = the duration of running up to that point.

examples.sentence: By the time they resolve this conflict, they will have been arguing for decades.
examples.translation: Resolution = future (hoped-for) moment. Decades = the depressing duration of the argument.

sections.title: Form: will + have + been + verb-ing
sections.content: Will have been + verb-ing for all subjects. This is a four-part structure. Contractions: I'll have been working. Negative: won't have been + verb-ing. Question: Will + subject + have + been + verb-ing?

examples.sentence: By retirement, she'll have been teaching for 35 years. That's remarkable.
examples.translation: Four parts: will + have + been + teaching. Retirement = future deadline.

examples.sentence: By midnight, I'll have been studying for twelve straight hours.
examples.translation: Midnight = future deadline. Twelve hours = the duration that will have accumulated.

comparisons.explanation: Future Perfect Simple = completed result by a deadline (how many, done). Future Perfect Continuous = duration of process by a deadline (how long, effort). If you can count things or focus on completion → Simple. If you want to show how long, how hard, how much effort → Continuous.

examples.sentence: By 5pm, I'll have written 20 emails. (Simple) — result: 20 emails exist, done.
examples.translation: Counting completed items — focus on achievement.

examples.sentence: By 5pm, I'll have been writing emails for 8 hours straight. (Continuous) — duration: 8 exhausting hours
examples.translation: Focus on effort and duration — how long I've been at it.

commonMistakes: By then, I will have been work here for a decade. ✗ → working ✓ (needs -ing form)
She will have been knew him for years. ✗ → She will have known him ✓ (know is stative — no Continuous)
